project.name: matomo
project.note: The vulnerable dependency exists in the screenshot-testing subproject, not the root matomo package.json. The root project also has json-schema@0.2.3 as a devDependency (via its own package-lock.json), but the screenshot-testing subproject has the deeper, more interesting chain.

vulnerability.description: Prototype pollution vulnerability in json-schema allows attackers to modify Object prototype properties via crafted JSON input, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution or denial of service.

dependency_chain.description: json-schema@0.2.3 is a transitive dependency buried 4 levels deep. It is pulled in through the classic request -> http-signature -> jsprim chain. The 'request' package is a direct dependency of matomo-screenshot-testing.

all_paths.description: Secondary path via 'request-promise' which has 'request' as a peerDependency (resolved to the same request@2.88.2 instance)

other_critical_vulns_considered.name: eventsource
other_critical_vulns_considered.reason_not_selected: Same depth but fewer siblings at each level; json-schema chain has richer branching (request has 18 direct deps vs react-dev-utils has 14)

other_critical_vulns_considered.name: koa
other_critical_vulns_considered.reason_not_selected: Only depth 2, too shallow for interesting DFS visualization

occurrences.note: jsprim declares json-schema@0.2.3 as a dependency

dfs_tree_context.description: DFS traversal context for visualizing the path to json-schema@0.2.3. At each level, siblings are the other dependencies of the parent node. During DFS, these siblings would be visited before backtracking up. The 'on_path' field marks the dependency that leads toward the vulnerable target.

children.name: chai-image-assert
children.note: leads to eventsource and koa chains

levels.dfs_note: DFS would explore children left-to-right. request is the 9th child; 8 siblings explored first before descending toward the target.
request has 20 direct dependencies. http-signature is the 9th; DFS explores 8 siblings before finding the path to json-schema. This is the widest node in the chain -- classic DFS branching challenge.

children.name: sshpk
children.note: sshpk itself has 9 children: asn1, assert-plus, bcrypt-pbkdf, dashdash, ecc-jsbn, getpass, jsbn, safer-buffer, tweetnacl

levels.dfs_note: http-signature has 3 children. jsprim is the 2nd; DFS would explore assert-plus first, then descend into jsprim. sshpk (3rd) has a deep subtree of 9 dependencies that would be explored on backtrack.

children.name: verror
children.note: verror has 3 children: assert-plus, core-util-is, extsprintf

levels.dfs_note: jsprim has 4 children. json-schema is the 3rd; DFS explores assert-plus and extsprintf first, then finds the vulnerable target. verror (4th) would never be visited in a target-search DFS that terminates on finding the target.
json-schema@0.2.3 is a leaf node with no dependencies. DFS terminates here -- target found.

dfs_traversal_summary.nodes_visited_before_target_naive_dfs: In a naive left-to-right DFS: 8 (level 0) + 8 (level 1) + 1 (level 2) + 2 (level 3) = 19 sibling nodes explored before reaching json-schema, not counting subtrees of those siblings.
dfs_traversal_summary.why_interesting_for_dfs: The request@2.88.2 node is a classic DFS challenge with 20 children (branching factor 20). A naive DFS would explore many dead-end subtrees (aws-sign2, aws4, caseless, etc.) before reaching http-signature. Combined with the 11-way branch at the root level, this chain creates a wide, deep tree that demonstrates the importance of pruning or heuristic ordering in DFS.
